_purpose: Cultural marker profile for the NY Hudson Valley cluster. Maps the cultural fingerprints that appear in records and family memory — Dutch cultural persistence, Huguenot stone houses, Palatine German traditions, the patroon manor system's social hierarchy, Hudson River School aesthetics, Shaker simplicity, and naming patterns including Dutch patronymics. The valley's cultural landscape is layered: Dutch underneath, English overlay, German and Huguenot enclaves, Yankee incursion from the east, and Irish and Italian communities built on canal and quarry work.
display_name: NY Hudson Valley — Cultural Marker Profile

denomination_map.origin_signal: Dutch Reformed membership is the single strongest indicator of Dutch colonial ancestry in the Hudson Valley. The church was established at Fort Orange (Albany) in 1642 and at New Amsterdam (New York City) in 1628. Congregations spread along the river corridor as Dutch settlement expanded. Records were kept in Dutch into the 1760s-1770s, and Dutch language worship continued in some congregations into the 1800s.
denomination_map.class_signal: In the colonial period, Dutch Reformed membership crossed all classes — from the Van Rensselaer patroons to their tenant farmers. After the English takeover, Dutch Reformed membership gradually became associated with the established Dutch-American gentry — the old families who held land, office, and social position. By the 1800s, Dutch Reformed was a marker of old-money respectability in the valley.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Dutch Reformed church records are among the oldest and best-preserved in North America. Albany's records begin in the 1640s. Baptismal records name both parents and sponsors (witnesses), providing kinship evidence. Marriage records name both parties and often their parents. Consistory minutes record disciplinary actions and admissions. Records held at the New York State Library, the Holland Society, and individual congregations. CRITICAL: Early records are in Dutch and require translation.
denomination_map.race_note: Enslaved people in Dutch households were sometimes baptized in the Dutch Reformed church. These baptismal records — where they exist — are among the very few documentary traces of enslaved individuals in the colonial Hudson Valley.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Lutheran and German Reformed membership signals Palatine German origin. The 1710 Palatine migration established these congregations in Germantown (Columbia), West Camp (Greene), and Rhinebeck (Dutchess). Records kept in German into the 1790s. The distinction between Lutheran and Reformed followed families from their Rhineland home parishes — a family that was Lutheran in the Palatinate remained Lutheran in New York.
denomination_map.class_signal: Palatine German families were tenant farmers and small freeholders — a step below the Dutch patroon families in the valley's social hierarchy. Lutheran or German Reformed membership in the Hudson Valley signals the industrious, church-going yeoman class of the German immigrant community.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: German church records frequently note the family's place of origin in the Palatinate, making them invaluable for tracing trans-Atlantic connections. Records held at congregations, the New York State Library, and the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America archives. The 1710 Kocherthal lists cross-reference with church records.

denomination_map.era_range: 1678-circa 1780 (merged into Dutch Reformed and other denominations)
denomination_map.origin_signal: French Reformed membership signals Huguenot refugee ancestry. Limited to the New Paltz community and its offshoots. Records in French into the 1730s. The congregation merged with the broader Reformed tradition by the late 1700s as Huguenot identity assimilated with the surrounding Dutch and English populations.
denomination_map.class_signal: The Huguenot founding families of New Paltz were landowners and community leaders. Huguenot Reformed membership in the valley signals the original patentee families and their descendants — a distinctive social elite in the New Paltz area.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Records held by the Huguenot Historical Society, New Paltz. French-language records require translation. The twelve founding families are extensively documented. Huguenot church records are some of the most genealogically productive documents for tracing refugee families because they were maintained with the precision of a persecuted community documenting its own survival.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Congregational or Presbyterian membership in the Hudson Valley's eastern counties signals New England Yankee origin. These denominations were dominant in Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Vermont, and Yankee settlers brought them across the Taconic hills. Their presence east of the river, in stark contrast to the Dutch Reformed congregations along the river, maps the boundary between the Dutch and Yankee cultural zones.
denomination_map.class_signal: Congregational membership signals the independent, educated yeoman class of New England origin — farmers who valued literacy, town-meeting governance, and an educated ministry. A clear cultural contrast with the Dutch tenant-farmer tradition.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Well-maintained records. New England church records at both origin (CT, MA, VT) and destination can document the migration chain. Transfer letters between congregations are direct evidence of family movement.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Catholic membership in the Hudson Valley signals Irish immigration (1825-1870) or Italian immigration (1880-1930). Before 1825, Catholic presence was negligible — the valley was overwhelmingly Protestant. The growth of Catholic parishes tracks the arrival of Irish canal and railroad workers, then Italian quarry and construction workers.
denomination_map.class_signal: In the nineteenth century, Catholic membership signaled working-class immigrant status — canal diggers, railroad builders, quarry workers. By the early twentieth century, Irish Catholics had moved into political power (Albany's Democratic machine) and the professional class, while Italian Catholics remained more concentrated in manual labor.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Parish records (baptism, marriage, death) are the primary genealogical source for Irish and Italian immigrant families. Catholic records are typically more complete and standardized than Protestant records in this period. Albany diocesan archives hold centralized records. Individual parish registers are often still at the parish or at the diocesan archives.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Quaker meeting membership in the Hudson Valley signals English Quaker settlement, particularly in Dutchess and Columbia counties. The Nine Partners Patent area (eastern Dutchess) was a significant Quaker settlement. Quakers played a notable role in the early abolitionist movement in the valley.
denomination_map.class_signal: Quaker families tended to be prosperous farmers and merchants. Meeting membership signals a family committed to plain living, literacy, and social conscience. Quakers were disproportionately represented in antislavery activity.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Quaker meeting records are among the best-maintained genealogical sources in America. Monthly meeting minutes, birth/death records, marriage certificates, and certificates of removal (transfer) provide continuous family documentation. Records at Haviland Records Room, New York Yearly Meeting, and Swarthmore College.
denomination_map.race_note: Quaker meetings were among the first to record and advocate for the rights of free Black community members. Some meeting records document manumissions of enslaved people by Quaker members.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Shaker membership signals participation in the utopian communal movement. The New Lebanon Shaker community (Columbia County, est. 1787) was the 'mother house' of American Shakerism — the administrative center of the entire movement. The Watervliet Shaker community (Albany County) was where Ann Lee settled in 1776.
denomination_map.class_signal: Shaker membership transcended class — converts came from all backgrounds. However, the communities attracted individuals seeking economic security, spiritual purpose, or escape from difficult circumstances (widows, orphans, failed farmers).
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Shaker communities maintained meticulous records — membership rolls, journals, account books, and correspondence. These are held at the Hancock Shaker Village, the New York State Museum, and the Western Reserve Historical Society. Because Shakers practiced celibacy, members' biological families are documented in pre-conversion records but the Shaker record itself does not generate descendants. Research often involves finding what happened to family members who did NOT join.

naming_conventions.description: Dutch naming in the Hudson Valley followed the patronymic tradition: a child's surname was formed from the father's given name plus '-sen' or '-son' (Jansen = Jan's son). The 'Van' prefix indicated a place of origin (Van Buren = from Buren, Netherlands; Van Cortlandt = from Kortland). The 'De' prefix was an article (De Witt = the white/fair one). This patronymic system meant that surnames could change every generation — a man named Pieter Jansen could have a son named Hendrik Pietersen. The system caused enormous genealogical confusion when families transitioned to fixed English-style surnames in the 1700s-1800s. A family that was 'Jansen' in one generation might become 'Johnson' in the next, or might fix 'Jansen' as a permanent surname.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The patronymic system is the single greatest challenge in Dutch Hudson Valley genealogy. A researcher who does not understand that Pieter Jansen's son could be recorded as Hendrik Pietersen will lose the family connection. The 'Van' prefix is the most reliable indicator of Dutch origin — if a surname begins with 'Van' in a Hudson Valley record, the family is almost certainly of Dutch descent. The transition to fixed surnames happened at different times in different families, creating a period (roughly 1700-1780) where both systems operated simultaneously.

naming_conventions.pattern: Huguenot French naming at New Paltz
naming_conventions.description: The Huguenot families at New Paltz used French given names in the first two generations: Abraham, Jean, Pierre, Louis, Andries (Andre), Simon. Surnames were fixed: Hasbrouck, Du Bois, Deyo, Lefevre, Bevier, Crispell, Freer, Elting. By the third generation, English given names began replacing French ones, but the distinctive surnames persisted. The tight intermarriage pattern among the twelve families means that Huguenot genealogy requires careful tracking of which Abraham Hasbrouck is which — there were many.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The small number of founding families and dense intermarriage means that Huguenot genealogy in New Paltz is both well-documented and confusing. The Huguenot Historical Society maintains comprehensive genealogical files. The French given names in early records are a reliable ethnic marker.

naming_conventions.description: Palatine German surnames were anglicized over two to three generations: Zimmerman became Simmons or Carpenter, Mueller became Miller, Schneider became Snyder, Schmidt became Smith, Weber became Weaver, Klein became Kline. German given names transitioned: Johann became John, Wilhelm became William, Katharina became Catherine. This anglicization creates a genealogical trap — a family recorded as 'Simmons' in the 1790 census may be the same family recorded as 'Zimmerman' in the 1710 Palatine lists.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The Palatine anglicization pattern is the second-greatest naming challenge in Hudson Valley genealogy (after Dutch patronymics). The Kocherthal/1710 subsistence lists provide the German originals; comparison with later census records reveals the anglicized forms. Researchers must be alert to multiple surname variants for the same family.

naming_conventions.description: Dutch families favored biblical and traditional Dutch names: Pieter, Jan, Hendrik, Cornelis, Dirck, Gerrit, Jacobus (male); Maritje, Jannetje, Catarina, Geertje, Annatje, Neeltje (female). The feminine diminutive '-tje' or '-je' suffix (Maritje, Jannetje) is a strong Dutch ethnic marker. Children were named for grandparents in a strict pattern: first son for paternal grandfather, first daughter for maternal grandmother, second son for maternal grandfather, second daughter for paternal grandmother.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The Dutch grandparent-naming pattern is a powerful genealogical tool. If a first son is named Cornelis, the paternal grandfather was almost certainly Cornelis. The '-tje' suffix on a woman's given name in a Hudson Valley record is a reliable indicator of Dutch descent. This naming pattern persisted through the mid-1800s in conservative Dutch families.

foodways.food_tradition: Oliebollen and Dutch New Year foods
foodways.context: Oliebollen (fried dough balls, ancestor of the American doughnut) were the traditional Dutch New Year's treat. Dutch families in the valley prepared them for the New Year's visiting tradition, when families traveled from house to house exchanging greetings and food. Other Dutch foods that persisted: koekjes (cookies — the word 'cookie' itself is Dutch), pannekoeken (pancakes), and wafel (waffles). The Dutch culinary tradition also contributed coleslaw (koolsla), and cruller (krullen).
foodways.class_signal: Dutch food traditions persisted longest in the established Dutch-American families — the Van Rensselaers, Schuylers, and their social circle. The traditions were markers of Dutch identity rather than class, but their persistence correlated with families who maintained Dutch language and church membership longest.
foodways.narrative_hook: On New Year's Day, {ancestor_name}'s family made oliebollen — the fried dough that the Dutch had brought from the Netherlands, rolled in sugar, served to every visitor who came through the door, a taste of Holland in a valley that had been English for over a century.

foodways.food_tradition: Apple culture — cider, apple butter, dried apples
foodways.context: The Hudson Valley was one of America's premier apple-growing regions. Cider was the universal beverage — consumed by men, women, and children, often lightly alcoholic ('hard cider'). Apple butter was a staple. Dried apples were a preserved food. The valley's orchards produced distinctive varieties: Northern Spy, Newtown Pippin, Baldwin, Esopus Spitzenburg (originally from Ulster County). Apple culture was so central that cider functioned as currency in some transactions.
foodways.class_signal: Orchard ownership was a measure of agricultural success. Families with productive orchards were better positioned economically than grain-only farmers. Cider quality reflected farming skill.
foodways.narrative_hook: Cider was the water of the valley — {ancestor_name} drank it at breakfast, dinner, and supper, pressed from Esopus Spitzenburg apples that had been named for the very ground the orchard stood on.

foodways.food_tradition: Palatine German sausage, sauerkraut, and bread traditions
foodways.context: Palatine German families maintained distinctive food traditions: sausage-making, sauerkraut (fermented cabbage), dense rye and wheat breads, and schmaltz (rendered fat). These traditions marked the German communities in Columbia, Greene, and Dutchess counties. German baking traditions influenced the broader valley — the valley's bakeries and bread quality reflected German immigrant skill.
foodways.class_signal: German food traditions were markers of ethnic identity rather than class. Their persistence in a family's practice indicated the strength of the German cultural connection.
foodways.narrative_hook: The sauerkraut crock in the cellar told the story — {ancestor_name}'s family still made it the way the Palatines had brought the recipe from the Rhineland, the fermented cabbage a taste of a homeland two generations and an ocean away.

dialect_markers.description: Dutch-influenced English persisted in the valley for over two centuries after the English takeover. Features included: 'stoop' (front porch, from Dutch 'stoep'), 'boss' (from Dutch 'baas'), 'cookie' (from 'koekje'), 'coleslaw' (from 'koolsla'), 'Santa Claus' (from 'Sinterklaas'), 'yankee' (possibly from 'Janke'). Place names preserved Dutch pronunciation: 'kill' for creek (from Dutch 'kil'), 'hook' for point (from 'hoek'), 'vliet' or 'fly' for swamp. Washington Irving's fictional characters Rip Van Winkle and Ichabod Crane captured and popularized the valley's Dutch cultural layer.
dialect_markers.class_signal: Dutch vocabulary in English speech marked old-family Hudson Valley identity. Using Dutch terms unselfconsciously was a marker of belonging to the established community rather than being a newcomer.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} sat on the stoop and called the creek a 'kill,' {pronoun_subject} was speaking a Hudson Valley English that still carried the Dutch — words that had crossed the Atlantic with the first settlers and refused to leave, even after the English flag had flown for two centuries.

dialect_markers.description: Palatine families who transitioned from German to English retained German vocabulary items: 'dumb' (meaning stupid, from German 'dumm'), 'smearcase' (cottage cheese, from 'Schmierkase'), 'hex' (witch, from 'Hexe'). Place names in German-settled areas preserve German: Germantown, Rhinebeck, Rhinecliff. German syntax influenced English sentence construction in some families — verb placement and compound word formation.
dialect_markers.class_signal: German vocabulary retention was an ethnic marker. Families that maintained German terms in English speech were more recently transitioned from German-language households.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: The neighbors still called the place Germantown though nobody had spoken German there in fifty years — the name was a fossil, like the sauerkraut recipe and the Lutheran church, marking what {ancestor_name}'s family had been before they became American.

social_hierarchies.hierarchy_name: The Hudson Valley patroon-tenant class system
social_hierarchies.description: The Hudson Valley had a landed aristocracy unique in American history. The patroon families — Van Rensselaer, Livingston, Philipse, Hardenbergh, Beekman — held patents of thousands to hundreds of thousands of acres. They did not sell the land; they leased it to tenant farmers on perpetual leases that required annual rent payments in wheat, labor (days of service), and a portion of any sale price when the tenant transferred the lease. Below the patroons were the commercial gentry — merchants, professionals, and smaller landowners in the river towns. Below them were the tenant farmers — families who worked the land for generations but never owned it. Below the tenants were the landless laborers and, until 1827, enslaved people. This hierarchy persisted from the 1630s until the Anti-Rent Wars of the 1840s finally broke the tenant system. (1) Patroon families and landed gentry. (2) River-town merchants, professionals, and millers. (3) Tenant farmers on the great estates. (4) Landless laborers, hired workers. (5) Enslaved people (until 1827).
social_hierarchies.narrative_hook: The Van Rensselaers owned the land, the merchants in {county_seat} traded on it, and {ancestor_name} farmed it for a rent that never ended — three centuries of hierarchy written into the deeds that {pronoun_subject} could not change and the patroon would not surrender.

social_hierarchies.hierarchy_name: Post-Civil War ethnic and occupational hierarchy
social_hierarchies.description: By the late nineteenth century, the valley's social hierarchy had restructured around ethnicity and occupation. Old Dutch-American families retained social prestige and landholding. Yankee-origin families (Washington, eastern Columbia) held comparable status. Irish families had moved from canal-labor outsiders to Democratic political insiders (Albany, Troy). Italian immigrants occupied the lowest rung of the white hierarchy — quarry workers and construction laborers. African American families, free since 1827, were a small but persistent presence confined to the lowest-status employment. The arrival of IBM in 1941 introduced a new class: the corporate professional, often a transplant from elsewhere, who disrupted the valley's hereditary social order.
social_hierarchies.narrative_hook: By {year}, the valley's old families had been joined by new ones — {ancestor_name} navigated a social order where the Dutch name on the mailbox carried weight, the Irish ran the city hall, the Italians cut the stone, and the IBM engineers were building something none of them understood.

material_culture.description: Dutch colonial architecture in the Hudson Valley is distinctive: stone houses with steep-pitched roofs, wide floorboards, Dutch doors (split horizontally), and jambless fireplaces. The houses were built of local limestone or fieldstone, with deep-set windows and thick walls. The Dutch barn — a large, H-frame timber structure with a central threshing floor — is architecturally unique to Dutch-settled areas. These buildings survive across the valley and are the most visible physical evidence of Dutch settlement. The Bronck House (Greene County, 1663) is one of the oldest surviving Dutch structures in New York.
material_culture.class_signal: Stone construction was more common among the established Dutch families — tenant farmers more often built frame houses. A stone house in the valley signals a family of substance in the colonial or early republic period.
material_culture.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name}'s stone house was built when the valley was still Dutch — limestone walls two feet thick, a Dutch door split in half, and a jambless fireplace that heated the room from the center. The house has outlived every family that lived in it.

material_culture.item: Huguenot stone houses at New Paltz
material_culture.description: The seven surviving Huguenot stone houses on Huguenot Street in New Paltz are the most concentrated group of colonial stone houses in North America. Built by the founding families (Hasbrouck, Du Bois, Deyo, Bevier, Freer, Lefevre), they are one-and-a-half to two-story limestone structures with steep roofs and deep cellars. They reflect the architectural traditions the Huguenots brought from France and the Rhineland.
material_culture.class_signal: The Huguenot stone houses were built by the founding patentee families — the elite of the New Paltz community. Stone construction at New Paltz signals first-generation Huguenot ancestry.
material_culture.narrative_hook: The stone house on Huguenot Street was built by {ancestor_name}'s people — French Protestants who had fled France, passed through the Rhineland, and built in stone because they intended to stay, and the house still stands three centuries later as proof they did.

material_culture.description: Shaker material culture from the New Lebanon and Watervliet communities is characterized by functional simplicity, fine craftsmanship, and absence of ornament. Shaker furniture (chairs, tables, cabinets), oval boxes, baskets, and seed packets were sold commercially and are now among the most valued examples of American folk art. The Shaker aesthetic — 'beauty rests on utility' — influenced broader American design.
material_culture.class_signal: Shaker goods in a non-Shaker household indicated commerce with the community. Shaker furniture was respected for its quality. Possession of Shaker-made goods is not an ethnic or class marker but a geographic one — proximity to New Lebanon or Watervliet.
material_culture.narrative_hook: The Shaker chair in {ancestor_name}'s kitchen came from the community at New Lebanon — made by believers who thought simplicity was a form of worship, the ladder-back so perfectly balanced it could hang from a peg rail, beauty reduced to function.

cultural_calendar.description: The Dutch New Year's visiting tradition was the most distinctive cultural event in the valley's Dutch communities. Families prepared oliebollen, cookies, and cider. Men and boys traveled from house to house paying respects and receiving food and drink. The tradition was universal among Dutch families into the early 1800s and survived in some families through the century. Washington Irving described the tradition in his Knickerbocker's History of New York (1809), which both documented and satirized Dutch Hudson Valley culture.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: New Year's visiting reinforced the Dutch community's social network. The order of visiting followed social hierarchy — the patroon family's house was visited first. The tradition's survival long after Dutch language loss demonstrated the cultural persistence of the Dutch community.

cultural_calendar.description: The Dutch Sinterklaas tradition — gift-giving by St. Nicholas on December 5-6 — was the original form of the American Christmas gift-giving tradition. In the Hudson Valley, Sinterklaas was the primary winter holiday celebration for Dutch families until the English Christmas tradition gradually merged with and absorbed it in the early 1800s. The American 'Santa Claus' derives directly from the Dutch 'Sinterklaas' of the Hudson Valley.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: Sinterklaas was the Dutch cultural equivalent of Christmas — and its transformation into the American Santa Claus tradition is one of the most significant cultural exports from the Hudson Valley to American life.

cultural_calendar.timing: Late May / early June (fifty days after Easter)
cultural_calendar.description: Pinkster was the Hudson Valley's most distinctive cultural festival — a multi-day celebration at Pentecost that combined Dutch Reformed religious observance with African American cultural expression. In Albany and other Dutch communities, enslaved and free Black residents celebrated Pinkster with music, dancing, and elected 'Pinkster kings' who presided over the festival. By the late 1700s, Pinkster had become the most visible African American cultural event in the valley. Albany's Common Council banned the festival in 1811, citing disorder, but its suppression was driven by anxiety about Black public celebration and autonomy.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: Pinkster is genealogically significant because it is one of the few documented public events where enslaved people in the Hudson Valley exercised cultural agency. The festival's suppression in 1811 reflects the racial anxieties of the early republic period. Any narrative involving Black ancestry in the colonial or early republic Hudson Valley should reference Pinkster.

cultural_calendar.description: The Anti-Rent movement produced its own calendar of protest events: rent-refusal days when tenants collectively refused to pay, 'calico Indian' gatherings where protesters disguised in calico and sheepskin masks confronted sheriffs serving eviction notices, and mass meetings. These events concentrated in Albany, Rensselaer, Columbia, and Delaware counties. The movement peaked in 1844-1845 and resulted in a new state constitution (1846) that prohibited feudal tenures.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: The Anti-Rent events are genealogically significant because they document which families participated in the resistance. Arrest records, trial transcripts, and newspaper coverage name individuals. A family's presence in Anti-Rent records indicates tenant status on one of the great patents and active resistance to the patroon system.

narrative_hooks: The 'Van' in {ancestor_name}'s surname was a map — it told you the family came from Holland, settled along the Hudson, and had been in the valley since before the English flag flew over Fort Orange.

The Dutch Reformed church in {county} County kept records in Dutch for over a century after England took the colony — {ancestor_name}'s baptism was recorded in a language the British government could not read.

Pinkster in Albany was the one week when Black residents of the valley owned the public square — {ancestor_name} danced, celebrated, and chose a Pinkster king in a festival the city eventually banned because freedom, even for a week, frightened the people who held the power.
The Huguenot stone houses at New Paltz have stood for three centuries — {ancestor_name}'s family built them to outlast the persecution that drove them from France, and the houses kept that promise.

The Anti-Rent Wars were {ancestor_name}'s revolution — tenants in calico disguises refusing to pay rent to a family that had collected it for two hundred years, demanding the right to own the land they had always worked.

Washington Irving wrote Rip Van Winkle to capture what {ancestor_name}'s valley felt like — a place where the Dutch past slept under the English present, and a man could wake up to find the world had changed while the mountains had not.
source_references: Shorto, Russell. The Island at the Center of the World. New York: Doubleday, 2004.
Goodfriend, Joyce D. Before the Melting Pot: Society and Culture in Colonial New York City. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1991.
Christman, Henry. Tin Horns and Calico: A Decisive Episode in the Emergence of Democracy. New York: Henry Holt, 1945.
Evers, Alf. The Catskills: From Wilderness to Woodstock. Garden City, NY: Doubleday, 1972.
White, Shane. 'Pinkster: Afro-Dutch Syncretization in New York City and the Hudson Valley.' Journal of American Folklore 102, no. 403 (1989): 68-75.
Kim, Sung Bok. Landlord and Tenant in Colonial New York: Manorial Society, 1664-1775. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1978.
LeFevre, Ralph. History of New Paltz, New York, and Its Old Families. Albany: Fort Orange Press, 1909.
